food_name: Pork Leg Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Gio Heo)
region_of_origin: Vietnam / Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Hu Tieu Gio Heo; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002700) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese pork leg noodle soup; A0CKP (soup, noodle-based) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific dish. No code assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Pork Leg Noodle Soup as typically served in a Singapore Vietnamese restaurant, weighing approximately 500 g and comprising broth, rice noodles, braised pork leg, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Hu Tieu (Rice Noodle Soup) — nutrient composition estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central component data for pork leg, rice noodles, and pork bone broth
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Hu Tieu Gio Heo as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 35% pork bone broth, 30% cooked rice noodles, 25% braised pork leg, and 10% vegetables and garnishes. Primary references: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table entries for pork broth, rice noodles (cooked), and pork leg (cooked); USDA SR Legacy entries for pork leg (cooked, braised) FDC ID 168359 and cooked rice noodles FDC ID 168917. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for a restaurant-prepared bowl.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (cooked) as analogous food; GI ~53 (low)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hu Tieu Gio Heo as a composite dish. GI estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked rice noodles, which have a reported GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The pork and broth components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 39 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 ≈ 20.7, rounded to 20.3 using per-100g carbohydrate of 7.8 g × 5 × 53 / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork bone broth, fish sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl provides approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the soup liquid consumed, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or fish sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles; low GI mitigates acute glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The rice noodle base has a low GI (~53), producing a moderate glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of noodles, favour a smaller noodle-to-broth ratio, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from braised pork leg and elevated sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised pork leg contributes saturated fat (~0.8 g per 100 g, ~4 g per serving) and the high-sodium broth raises cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency, choose leaner cuts where available, and avoid drinking the full volume of broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Leg Noodle Soup, known in Vietnamese as Hủ Tiếu Giò Heo (romanized: Hu Tieu Gio Heo), is a Vietnamese-origin noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurants. The dish consists of a clear, savoury pork bone broth simmered for several hours with aromatics including garlic, shallots, and dried squid or shrimp, into which soft rice noodles (hu tieu) and tender braised or slow-cooked pork leg (gio heo) are placed. The pork leg is typically braised until the skin becomes gelatinous and the meat falls easily from the bone, yielding a rich, collagen-laden texture. The broth is seasoned with fish sauce and soy sauce, giving it a deeply umami, slightly salty flavour profile. Garnishes typically include bean sprouts, spring onions, fried shallots, fresh chilli, and lime wedges. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~360 kcal per 500 g bowl), provides meaningful protein (~29 g per serving) from the pork leg, and derives its carbohydrate content primarily from the rice noodles (~39 g per serving). The sodium content is high (~2100 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The rice noodle base has a low glycemic index (~53), which moderates the postprandial glucose response, though the total glycemic load per serving is moderate (~20). The dish is dairy-free and gluten content depends on the soy sauce used. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Hu Tieu Gio Heo as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients (pork leg braised, cooked rice noodles, pork bone broth, vegetables). GI is estimated from the analogous food (cooked rice noodles) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved references and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per-100g estimates derived from weighted component analysis of the dish's constituent ingredients using FAO/INFOODS SEA (2020) and USDA SR Legacy approved sources. No single composite dish entry for Hu Tieu Gio Heo was found in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium content is estimated at 420 mg per 100 g (2100 mg per 500 g serving), reflecting typical restaurant preparation with fish sauce, soy sauce, and salted broth; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA component sources used for this estimation. 6. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for braised pork leg and cooked rice noodles — no industrial trans fats are present in this traditionally prepared dish. 7. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. 8. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road, Singapore, as identified in the GrabFood source record (GD002111). 9. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains pork and is therefore not halal.
